reason:
The statement 'The hierarchy produces SDP relaxations whose feasible sets are again constrained state spaces of finitely-generated *-algebras' is imprecise and potentially incorrect at finite level.

At a finite level k of the Klep-Magron-Volcic state polynomial hierarchy, the feasible set is concretely an SDP spectrahedron: a set of positive semidefinite moment matrices satisfying linear constraints. This is NOT literally a 'constrained state space of a finitely-generated *-algebra' without substantial additional argument. Specifically:

(1) The SDP relaxation at level k involves a truncated moment matrix indexed by words up to degree k. The feasible set is a convex subset of PSD matrices of some size N_k. To interpret this as a 'constrained state space of a *-algebra', one needs to identify a specific *-algebra A_k such that PSD moment matrices at level k correspond bijectively to states on A_k. The natural candidate is the quotient of the free *-algebra by the kernel of the moment map, but this algebra depends on k and its structure is not specified.

(2) Even if such an algebra A_k exists, one must verify it is a unital *-algebra (or Archimedean order unit space) satisfying the hypotheses of the main theorem. In particular, the Archimedean condition must hold.

(3) The Klep-Magron-Volcic framework introduces 'state polynomials' that generalize trace polynomials. The feasible set at level k is a spectrahedron. Whether this spectrahedron is canonically a constrained state space of some *-algebra requires explicit verification.

Without explicitly identifying A_k and verifying these conditions, the claim that 'our theorem applies directly' is unjustified.
